stories.headline: Prahaar Teaser: Rajkummar Rao Plays Ujjwal Nikam, Demands Ajmal Kasab's Hanging
stories.what: The teaser for Prahaar has been released, featuring Rajkummar Rao in the role of celebrated public prosecutor Ujjwal Nikam, who famously led the prosecution in the 26/11 Mumbai attacks case and sought the death penalty for terrorist Ajmal Kasab. The footage shows Rao embodying Nikam's courtroom intensity as he demands Kasab's hanging. The film revisits one of the most consequential legal battles in post-Independence India, bringing a pivotal chapter of contemporary history to the big screen.
stories.lens: A gripping teaser of a film centred on one of India's most high-profile terrorism prosecutions marks Rajkummar Rao's boldest real-life transformation yet — and revives memory of 26/11 for a new audience.
stories.article:
Rajkummar Rao steps into the shoes of renowned public prosecutor Ujjwal Nikam in the teaser for Prahaar, a film that dramatises the prosecution of Ajmal Kasab, the lone surviving gunman from the 26 November 2008 Mumbai terrorist attacks.

The teaser presents Rao in a commanding courtroom avatar, voicing Nikam's historic demand for Kasab's hanging — a moment that gripped the nation as the legal proceedings following the attacks unfolded. Ujjwal Nikam, Maharashtra's most high-profile special public prosecutor, was the face of the state's case against Kasab, who was eventually convicted and executed in 2012.

The choice of Rajkummar Rao for the role is significant. Known for physically and emotionally transformative performances, Rao appears to have immersed himself in Nikam's mannerisms and bearing. The film's premise puts it squarely in the tradition of Indian courtroom dramas anchored in real events, a genre that has seen renewed audience interest in recent years.

Prahaar arrives at a time when content rooted in true crime and national security events continues to draw strong viewership both in theatres and on streaming platforms. The teaser's release has already generated considerable conversation online, with audiences and critics responding to the weight of the subject matter and Rao's apparent intensity in the role.
key_facts: Rajkummar Rao plays real-life prosecutor Ujjwal Nikam in Prahaar
Film centres on the prosecution of 26/11 attacker Ajmal Kasab
Kasab demanded death penalty — Nikam led the case to conviction

stories.headline: Businessman Found Dead at Actor Krishi Thapanda's Bengaluru Home
stories.what: A businessman was found dead at the Bengaluru residence of Kannada actor Krishi Thapanda, triggering a police investigation. The circumstances surrounding the death are currently under scrutiny, with authorities examining whether foul play was involved. The incident has sent shockwaves through the Kannada film industry and raised questions about the events leading up to the discovery.
stories.lens: A death at a film star's home in Bengaluru puts the spotlight on the circumstances and brings law enforcement directly into the entertainment world.
stories.article:
A businessman was found dead at the Bengaluru home of Kannada actor Krishi Thapanda, police confirmed on Tuesday, setting off a criminal investigation into the incident.

Authorities reached the actor's residence after being alerted to the discovery. The identity of the deceased businessman has been noted by investigators, though the exact cause of death and the sequence of events leading up to it remained unclear at the time of reporting. Police are examining all angles, including the possibility of foul play.

Krishi Thapanda is a known face in the Kannada film industry, and the incident has drawn immediate attention from both media and the public. The nature of the businessman's connection to the actor and what brought him to the residence are among the central questions police are working to answer.

The case has created considerable unease within the Bengaluru entertainment community. Investigators are expected to question relevant individuals as the probe deepens. Further details are likely to emerge as the police investigation progresses.
key_facts: Businessman found dead at Kannada actor Krishi Thapanda's Bengaluru home
Police investigation launched into circumstances of death
Krishi Thapanda is a prominent figure in Kannada cinema

stories.headline: How OTT is Reimagining '90s Leading Ladies: Sonali Bendre, Madhuri Dixit, Karisma Kapoor
stories.what: A new wave of OTT productions is reviving and reframing the careers of Bollywood's biggest female stars from the 1990s — Sonali Bendre, Madhuri Dixit and Karisma Kapoor among them. Streaming platforms are offering these actresses roles with greater depth and complexity than the commercial Hindi film industry typically gave them in their peak years, allowing them to reinvent themselves for contemporary audiences. The trend reflects both the democratisation of storytelling through digital platforms and a growing appetite for women-led narratives in Indian entertainment.
stories.lens: For millions of viewers who grew up watching these stars, their OTT reinventions represent both nostalgia and a genuine reckoning with how Indian cinema has historically underused its women.
stories.article:
Three decades after they ruled the Hindi film industry, Sonali Bendre, Madhuri Dixit and Karisma Kapoor are finding new, more complex creative lives on streaming platforms — and audiences are taking notice.

OTT platforms have emerged as an unlikely second act for the biggest female stars of 1990s Bollywood. Where the commercial cinema of that era often cast these women in song-and-dance roles with limited dramatic range, streaming productions are giving them the space to inhabit layered, morally complex characters. Madhuri Dixit's work on Netflix's Finding Anamika, Karisma Kapoor's turn in Mentalhood, and Sonali Bendre's appearances in digital projects have all been cited as evidence of this transformation.

The phenomenon speaks to a structural shift in Indian entertainment. OTT platforms, competing fiercely for subscribers, have an incentive to develop content that appeals across age groups — and that includes viewers who grew up idolising these stars and are now eager to see them in stories that reflect adult realities. The stars themselves have spoken about valuing creative freedom over commercial formulas.

For film historians and gender critics, the trend also raises a pointed question: why did it take streaming to unlock the full potential of actresses who were always capable of more? The answer, many suggest, lies in the patriarchal structures of mainstream Bollywood that OTT, for all its imperfections, is quietly beginning to dismantle.
key_facts: Sonali Bendre, Madhuri Dixit and Karisma Kapoor finding renewed careers on OTT
Streaming platforms offering more complex roles than 1990s Bollywood did
Trend reflects growing appetite for women-led narratives in Indian streaming

stories.headline: Soni Razdan Defends Alia Bhatt: 'Not Her Fault She's Daughter of Industry Insiders'
stories.what: Actor Soni Razdan has come out in defence of her daughter Alia Bhatt, pushing back against the nepotism narrative that has long shadowed the actress's career. Razdan argued that it is not Alia's fault that she was born into a family with deep connections to the Hindi film industry, and that the actress should not be penalised for circumstances of birth she had no control over. The remarks reignite a debate that has been central to Bollywood's public discourse for years, particularly since the death of Sushant Singh Rajput brought questions of industry access and privilege to a national boil.
stories.lens: The nepotism debate in Bollywood has shaped public perception of several major stars — Soni Razdan's defence of Alia Bhatt keeps that conversation alive and asks a genuinely difficult question about fairness and talent.
stories.article:
Soni Razdan has addressed one of Bollywood's most persistent controversies head-on, arguing that her daughter Alia Bhatt cannot fairly be blamed for being born into a family of film industry insiders.

Razdan's remarks push back against the nepotism discourse that has trailed Alia Bhatt since the actress's debut. The argument is straightforward: no one chooses their parents or the advantages those parents confer, and penalising someone for a circumstance of birth is itself a form of unfairness. Razdan is a respected actress in her own right, and her willingness to speak publicly on the subject adds a maternal as well as an industry-insider perspective to a debate that often lacks nuance.

Alia Bhatt has, in recent years, built a body of work — including critically lauded performances in films like Gangubai Kathiawadi and Raazi — that many argue stands entirely on its own merits. Supporters contend that whatever door her surname opened, she has delivered the goods once inside. Critics, however, maintain that the structural advantages of a famous family background mean that equally talented actors from outside the industry never get the same door to open.

The nepotism debate in Bollywood shows little sign of fading. Soni Razdan's comments are unlikely to settle the argument, but they do add a dimension often missing from public discussion: the perspective of those inside the equation who believe the framing of inherited privilege misses the complexity of individual achievement.
key_facts: Soni Razdan says it's not Alia Bhatt's fault she was born to industry insiders
Alia Bhatt has been a frequent target of Bollywood nepotism criticism
Razdan is herself a veteran Bollywood actress
